observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the following:
a) The Municipal Accountant and GSO-designate to reconcile their records on
PPE and address the unreconciled difference of ₱50,860,390.66;
b) Municipal Accountant to examine the Report on Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE), particularly those PPEs
reported in the physical count, and ensure that those PPEs are supported
with subsidiary ledger cards and recorded in the books; and
c) GSO-designate, and in coordination with the Inventory Committee and
Municipal Engineer for local road network:
1) Conduct an annual and complete physical inventory of all PPE items,
including local road networks and infrastructure assets;
2) Regularly update its records and prepare and maintain PCs for each
class of PPE to record the acquisition, description, custody, disposal, and
other information about the asset.

We recommended that Management direct the following:
a) The Municipal Accountant to prospectively:
1. Ensure that there is RIS and SSMI together with distribution sheets and
pictures that would substantiate the issuance and distribution of
inventories before adjusting it to the appropriate expense account in the
books;
2. Request from GSO-designate an inventory count of current inventories
on hand and reconcile it with the records to adjust the purchases that
were treated as outright expenses and restore it in the inventory account
if there are still remaining inventories on hand; and
3. Maintain a supplies ledger cards per commodity/stock as required by the
guidelines and periodically reconcile it with the stock cards and property
cards to be maintained by the GSO for checks and balances;
b) The GSO- designate to prospectively:
1. Maintain the property cards and stock cards and periodically reconcile it
with the ledger cards by the Accounting Department; and
2. Ensure that requisition of stocks is supported with RIS for supplies
carried in stock or PR for supplies not carried in stock, as appropriate
and summarize it into SSMI weekly to be submitted to the Accounting
Office for the drawing of appropriate entries;
c) The Inventory Committee, immediately conduct the physical count of
Inventory particularly on the non- moving inventory of ₱9,840,539.00; and
submit the same to the Audit Team for verification; and
d) The GSO- Designate, Inventory Committee and Municipal Accounting
Office, in consideration of the physical count conducted, particularly on the
₱9,840,539.00, to initiate proper action to be taken, like expensing of
inventories if goods were already distributed; otherwise, if still in existence,
submit a written justification or documentation that indeed those were still
present and still in custody and copy furnished the Audit Team; and
e) The Inventory Committee to conduct the semestral count and prepare and
submit the RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31
of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the cash shortage by the
former Municipal Treasurer of ₱512,844.21 to the proper FS account, Due
from Officers and Employees, and provide the adequate disclosure on the
Notes to Financial Statements in order to achieve the fair presentation of the
Cash-Local Treasury account prospectively; and
b) Act on the cash shortage incurred by the former Municipal Treasurer by
enforcing collection and/or securing copy of the court decision through
formal correspondence.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to trace
and retrieve the original source documents to establish the validity of non-moving
claims aged two years and more totaling ₱2,516,624.15, and, where appropriate and
duly substantiated, make payments to identified payees to settle those; otherwise,
draw the necessary reversing, adjusting and/or correcting entries to de-recognize the
unsubstantiated claims.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the transactions totaling
₱516,616.39 that were erroneously recorded under the Other Payables
account and provide the adequate disclosure on the Notes to Financial
Statements in order to achieve the fair presentation of the said account;
b) Initiate retrieval efforts of the source documents and/or issue blind
confirmation letters to identified payees of the non-moving payables totaling
₱454,126.01 in order to establish the LGU obligations to settle them with
documentation on the LGUs efforts of retrieval; otherwise, consider
drawing an adjusting entry to equity;
c) Also consider referring the matter on the non-moving payables of the LGU
to the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), in accordance with
Item 10.0 of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2021; and
d) Assess the nature and existence of the other transactions contained in the
Other Payables account, and if warranted, also effect necessary adjusting
entry.

We recommended that Management:
a) Immediately direct the concerned finance and implementing LGU offices to
exert collaborative effort in the preparation of Loan Utilization Report as of
December 31, 2024, commencing from the approval date of the term loan
agreement;
b) Also direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the consolidated, editable
version of the bank loan release schedules; and
c) Ensure that internal control is in place for the LGU loan/s to ensure that
these loans are effectively managed, government resources are safeguarded
from wastage, prudence is exercised in contracting future loan agreements,
and the Loans Payable account is fairly presented in the financial
statements.

We recommended that Management:
a) Evaluate in the planning process the prioritization of PPAs to be charged to
the Local Development Fund particularly the school-related expenses which
should be appropriately charged to the annual Special Education Fund
(SEF), in compliance with Section 272 of the Local Government Code;
b) Present copy of authorization for the excess cost of ₱1,052,500.00 incurred
to purchase the bulldozer and submit a valid written justification why the
aforesaid transaction should not be disallowed in audit on the ground of
irregularity and/or lack of legal basis;
c) Immediately revert the amount of ₱1,052,500.00 to the Trust Fund-
LDRRMF account as part of the unexpended DRRM balance in CY 2024;
d) Stop inter-fund transfers and merging of purposes between the special LGU
funds, such as the 20 per cent Development Funds (DF) and Local Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF);
e) Also stop merging different transactions in one disbursement
process/voucher without specifying their corresponding appropriations,
despite the BAC’s adoption of procurement by lot; and
f) Optimally utilize the DF by prioritizing development projects, programs
and activities (PPAs) and by ensuring that these PPAs are “well-planned and
procurement-and-implementation-ready.”

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Engineering Office to prepare and maintain a monthly
GPPA Report for all LGU projects that are completed, on-going and for
implementation in CY 2025 and onwards; and (b) Ensure LGU’s
compliance with the prescribed format of the project signboards and inform
COA thereof.
b) We likewise recommended that Management immediately submit to the
Audit Team the following, together with written justification for the delays
and other related procurement and implementation documents to forestall
the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance:
1. The overall plan on the implementation of the Construction of new RHU
Building with target timelines and specific financing of this entire
development project; and
2. The copy of the approved time extensions for the Road Opening (Phase
2), Hobo-Timbang, along with the grounds cited for the request and
subsequent approval/disapproval thereof to support LGU’s non-
withholding of liquidated damages.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal
Engineer to implement a sound and effective monitoring system in place to compel
contractor’s compliance of the requirements of Section 62 of the RIRR of RA 9184 in
order to ensure that the procuring entity will be reimbursed of its expenses in the
restoration or reconstruction works in case of structural defect/failure occurring
during the applicable warranty period.

We recommended that Management ensure that the retention money equivalent
to five or 10 percent of the contract cost of the infrastructure project is properly
withheld and retained from the gross progress and/or final payments, and, should
retention money be released before final acceptance, ensure that both conditions are
present: (1) the covered project is satisfactorily done and on schedule, and (2) there is
proper substitution of surety bond in the equivalent amount.
Likewise, we recommended that Management prospectively prepare and attach
a Statement of Time Elapsed on the progress and final payments for all infrastructure
transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a) Require the Municipal Engineer and/or concerned authorized agency
personnel to supervise the implementation of the infrastructure projects,
and submit to the audit team copies of the available Results of Test
Analyses/Status of Quality Control Test complete with certified xerox copy
of official receipt required by the aforementioned rules and regulations in
order to ensure that the completed projects conforms with agency standards,
plans and specifications as part of its quality control program;
b) Require the Municipal Engineer/Bids and Awards Committee to include
Minimum Materials Testing Equipment in the Technical Component of the
Bid together with the other major equipment as contained in the Technical
Envelope, which shall be subjected to preliminary examination and further
verified during the post-qualification stage; and
c) Henceforth, require the Municipal Accountant not to pass during pre-audit
of the transaction particularly on final payment, disbursement vouchers on
infrastructure projects not supported with Results of Test Analyses from
DPWH/BRS/DOST/DTI accredited testing laboratories for construction
materials and concrete, which require testing to avoid suspensions and/or
disallowances in audit.

We recommended that Management:
a) Adhere strictly with Section 2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 as well as the
procurement rules and procedures of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and
strengthen the internal control system on procurements;
b) Strictly monitor the usage of fuel for motor vehicles owned by the
Municipality by implementing sound internal control in the requisition of
fuel as well as use thereof;
c) Strictly implement requiring the use serially numbered trip tickets and
strictly require the drivers to secure duly accomplished and approved trip
tickets before using the motor vehicles;
d) Implement logbook or its equivalent on the use of motor vehicles using the
serial numbers of trip tickets as reference to monitor the leave and return of
vehicles in the office premise; and
e) Require the GSO-designate to monitor the usage of fuel through the
approved Requisition and Issue Voucher for its issuance coupled with the
driver’s trip ticket as well as summary monthly travel and monthly fuel
consumption for submission to the Accounting Office as basis for the
expensing of Fuel.

We recommended that Management:
a) Immediately submit the copy of the required “adjusted GPB” for CY 2024
attached to a letter informing the DILG Provincial Office and the latter’s
acknowledgment receipt thereof;
b) Direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to ensure effective GAD
planning and budget to avoid adjustments during the year of
implementation; and
c) Prospectively comply with Section 4.1.C.7 (2) of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA
JMC No. 2013-01, as amended, if there are any changes and/or additions to
its DILG-endorsed GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) for CY 2025 and onwards;
otherwise, the GAD expenses that exceed their corresponding approved
budgets may be issued with Notice of Suspension or Disallowance.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) Determine the accurate year-end amount of the unexpended balance of the
1st Tranche of the Philippine Multi-Sectoral Nutrition Program (PMNP)
Performance-Based Grant (PBG) transferred by the DOH-CHD Field
Office No. V in CY 2024;
b) Based on the amount arrived at in the preceding step, prepare adjusting
journal entries necessary to record the amount under the appropriate
liability and equity accounts as prescribed in Chapter 10 of the PMNP
Project Operations Manual; and
c) Recognize the receipt of the next tranches of PBG, if any, as an outright
income, and the subsequent utilization thereof under appropriate asset and
expense accounts in compliance with the provisions of the Operations
Manual.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
Update its records and determine the components of unidentified PPE items reported in the books. If possible, trace the previous balance in the subsidiary ledger to gradually trace the unidentified PPE; and
Prepare and maintain PPELCs for each class of PPE to record the acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, depreciation, disposal and other information about the PPE, and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger.
GSO Designate in coordination with the Inventory Committee to:
Conduct an annual physical inventory of all PPE items, and report the quantity of items or articles per physical count under the ‘On hand per count’ column, and reflect any shortages or overages under the ‘Shortage/Overage’ column, if any;
Update its records and prepare RPCPPE and RPCLRN following the format prescribed in Annex 49 of NGAS for LGUs, and Annex C of COA Circular 2015-008, respectively; and
Prepare and maintain PCs for each class of PPE to record the acquisition, description, custody, disposal and other information about the asset.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Restate the reported balances of the accounts affected by prior period errors in the comparative FS, and provide a disclosure thereof in the Notes to FS, in accordance with the provisions of IPSAS 3;
Establish proper inventory cut-off, and coordinate with the MDRRMO, and other offices in custody of inventories, for the prompt and regular submission of distribution lists so that expenses are recorded in the proper period; and
Likewise, we recommend that Management direct the MDRRMO to submit the distribution lists for relief goods to the Accounting Office immediately after its distribution, or at year-end as an established cut- off so that related asset and expense accounts reflects its corrected balance in the period to which they relate.

We recommended that Management direct the:
Municipal Engineer, and other Implementing Offices to provide for the reason(s) for the non-completion and/or non-implementation of the identified priority projects above;
Municipal Development Council to ensure that PPAs in the Local Development Plan are supported with the necessary in-depth study and planning documents before its inclusion in the Plan to warrant the possible attainment of the project within the year; and
Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, and other Implementing Offices, to ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund through the completion and accomplishment of the priority projects identified in the Local Development Plan.

We recommended that Management, through the Municipal Treasurer:
Ensure that all collections are properly receipted and reflected in the RCD, and deposited daily and intact to depository banks, in compliance with the pertinent provisions of NGAS for LGUs and COA Circular; and
Stop using the collections for payment of any expenses as this will be covered appropriately by issuance of checks, or through the LGU’s established Petty Cash Fund, as appropriate.
Likewise, we recommended that Management consider designating a special disbursing officer, apart from the acting MT who already has a fair share of duties and responsibilities, for special cash advances so that its utilization and subsequent liquidation are properly and immediately managed.

We recommended that Management refrain from resorting to Negotiated Procurement-Emergency Cases for catering services or other procurements wherein no emergency is sought to be addressed and strictly adhere to the provisions of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184 prescribing the guidelines on procurement.

We recommended that Management:
Require the head of various offices to oversee the conduct of physical count of existing PPEs found in their respective offices and prepare the report using Annex A of the cited Circular for verification of the Inventory Committee;
Direct the GSO Designate, in coordination with the Inventory Committee, to update the RPCPPE following the prescribed format and using the data provided by various offices to reflect the actual count and value of the PPE items maintained by the Municipality; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain PPELCs for all items of PPE and ensure that the total balance of PPELCs tally with the balances of controlling PPE accounts in the General Ledger.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor issue a policy to stop the manual payment of bonuses and incentives of the employees and job orders, instead, it be made through the same existing Automated Payroll System of paying monthly salaries and wages facilitating the direct crediting to employees’ bank accounts to prevent delay and avoid the risk of loss or misappropriation of public funds that are inherent in a manual system of disbursement.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize depreciation expense for all depreciable assets, including the depreciable components of infrastructure assets to present the correct valuation of the PPE accounts; and
Record the depreciation expense for infrastructure assets and report any adjustments in the ensuing year to reflect the correct balance of accumulated depreciation for each item of PPE.

We recommended that Management direct the GFPS to:
Submit its GPB to the DILG within the prescribed period to enable the latter to review the same and effect the results of the review of the GPB to attain a more gender responsive PPAs; and
Maximize the utilization of GAD funds and monitor implementation of GAD PPAs so that no activity is left unimplemented at the end of the year and the intended benefits that could be derived from implementation of GAD related PPAs in the fund could be achieved.

We recommended that management:
Prepare a clear, comprehensive and well defined implementing guidelines on the LGU AICs program and all other financial assistance and institutionalize the same to formalize its adoption to ensure that only qualified and eligible indigent beneficiaries were given the type, kind and amount of assistance they needed after the assessment, validation and recommendation of the MSWDO; and
Initiate a comprehensive review of the LGUs Program on AICS and all other assistance to the poor, vulnerable and marginalized individuals and families so that the LGUs AICs and other assistance program attains its purpose and its benefits redound to as many qualified and needy clients the fund can accommodate.

We recommended that Management:
Convene with the LSB and agree on the timeline of the preparation and approval of the SEF budget following the indicative period of relevant activities in the cited Joint Circular;
Direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to work together in the preparation of the certified statement of SEF income to properly establish a more realistic basis of the available fund for appropriation; and
Remind the LSB to review the budget and its components to ensure that the fund will be utilized for priority programs and projects and will be prepared in accordance with the requirements of the relevant laws, and regulations.
